QKRI History — Jan to Jul 2026
KC_01 LNG Supply Disruption — UNIQUE KILL CONDITION (weight=0.30)
Why KC_01 is Qatar's Defining Risk — The Hormuz Dependency

Qatar operates as the world's largest single LNG exporter — QatarEnergy produces 77 Mtpa (million tonnes per annum) of LNG, supplying approximately 25% of global LNG trade. This output transits the Strait of Hormuz — a 33km minimum-width chokepoint shared with Iran. There is no alternative export route for Qatari LNG.

A Hormuz closure or QatarEnergy production halt would remove 77 Mtpa from global supply within days — equivalent to 5× Australia's Gorgon LNG output. Global LNG markets have no immediate substitute: the next largest single supplier (Australia's combined capacity ~88 Mtpa) is fragmented across 8+ projects and cannot surge output on short notice.

The North Field South (NFS) expansion (approved 2022; $28.7B FID) targets 126 Mtpa by 2030, further concentrating global LNG dependency on Qatar. This expansion deepens the Hormuz risk: more Qatar LNG = more global exposure to a single chokepoint.

Q3 2026 Status: NORMAL — No Hormuz disruption active. KC_01 score: 0.20. NFS Phase 1 ramp ongoing; shipping lanes clear.
KC_04 US Military Base — SINGLE TRIGGER (weight=0.15)
Al Udeid AFB — US CENTCOM Forward HQ

Al Udeid Air Base hosts the US Central Command (CENTCOM) forward HQ — the largest US air base in the Middle East, with 10,000+ US and coalition personnel. Al Udeid is Qatar's primary security guarantee and geopolitical anchor.

SINGLE TRIGGER LOGIC: If US military withdrawal from Al Udeid or a confirmed threat to the base is detected, QKRI is immediately floored at ELEVATED (0.60) regardless of the composite QKRI score. This is because:
(1) Qatar's security architecture collapses without US presence, immediately elevating all other KC risks; (2) Iran's behavior changes substantially without the US deterrent at Al Udeid; (3) This signal is low-probability but extremely high-impact.

Notably, during the 2017-2021 blockade, the US kept Al Udeid operational and even expanded Qatar-US military cooperation — demonstrating the base's strategic independence from Gulf political tensions.

US Stock Transmission Channels — 9 Equities
INVERSE BULLISH LOGIC — Cheniere Energy (LNG) & Cheniere Partners (CQP)

When KC_01 fires (Qatar LNG disrupted), Cheniere Energy (LNG) and Cheniere Partners (CQP) become INVERSE BULLISH: the disruption score is inverted (score = 1.0 − disruption_score) because US LNG fills the Qatar gap. At disruption_score=0.85, LNG/CQP inverse impact = +0.15 × sensitivity = significant bullish. This is Qatar's unique US equity transmission: no other canary in the network generates an INVERSE BULLISH signal for a US exporter.

Supply Chain Risk Intelligence
Chokepoint · Freight Rate · Critical Minerals · Nearshoring · Logistics
SCRI  0.3000  WATCH

Qatar Central Bank operates a emerging, mid-tier supply chain network, with growing logistics infrastructure but limited redundancy — single-port dependencies and thin inventory buffers amplify chokepoint shocks. Maritime chokepoint exposure is concentrated across: Strait of Hormuz (critical dependency, weight 0.95); Bab-el-Mandeb (moderate dependency, weight 0.30).

Critical import dependencies include food commodities, industrial machinery, consumer electronics. Semiconductor and critical mineral import dependency exposes the sovereign to US-China technology decoupling and East Asian supply chain disruptions.

Primary exports — LNG, crude oil, petrochemicals — are sensitive to global freight rate movements and trade route disruptions.

SCRI 0.3000 WATCH — Structural supply chain sensitivity is elevated. Chokepoint and critical import dependencies warrant close monitoring. A single trigger event (Strait closure, export ban, major port strike) could escalate to ELEVATED within days.

Watch signals: Strait of Hormuz closure or capacity restriction → immediate freight rate spike  |  SCFI/WCI/BDI freight index spike above 2-standard-deviation threshold → freight stress trigger

Real Estate Risk Intelligence
Property Price · Mortgage Stress · Commercial RE · Developer Debt · REIT
RERI  0.1300  NORMAL

Qatar Central Bank is classified as a emerging-market real estate market. Primary stress vector is residential affordability. Commercial real estate exposure is moderate CRE exposure with manageable vacancy pressures.

As an emerging market, Qatar real estate is exposed to dual risks: domestic credit tightening and external capital outflows. Currency depreciation compounds mortgage stress for dollar-denominated property loans.

No active developer stress flagged. Property developers in Qatar are not currently assessed as in distress at a systemic level.

RERI 0.1300 NORMAL — Property market stable at structural baseline. No systemic stress indicators active.

Watch signals: Property price index 3-month decline exceeding 2% → KC_RE01 trigger  |  Capital outflow episode → property market freeze and liquidity withdrawal

Insurance Risk Intelligence
Natural Catastrophe · Reinsurance · Property Withdrawal · Financial Guaranty · Climate
INRI  0.2600  WATCH

Qatar Central Bank faces 1 identified primary natural catastrophe perils: Drought Heatwave.

The insurance market in Qatar is shallow, with limited domestic capacity and heavy reliance on offshore reinsurance. Reinsurance profile: high reinsurance dependency — most catastrophic risk ceded offshore.

Climate risk classification: Tier 3 — high climate vulnerability; physical and transition risks are material and accelerating. Physical climate risks — including intensifying storm frequency, sea level rise, and heat stress on agriculture — are expected to materially increase insured losses over the medium term, potentially triggering insurer withdrawal from high-risk zones and creating insurance coverage gaps.

INRI 0.2600 WATCH — Insurance market faces structural vulnerabilities. Nat-cat exposure and/or reinsurance dependency requires monitoring. A major catastrophic event could expose coverage gaps rapidly.

Watch signals: Major Drought Heatwave event → test of reinsurance capacity and coverage adequacy  |  Global reinsurance market hardening (Jan or July renewals) → premium spike or capacity withdrawal  |  Insurer non-renewal notices in high-risk zones → coverage gap emergence  |  Sovereign disaster declaration without insurance backstop → fiscal cliff  |  Cat bond spread widening → forward signal of reinsurance market stress
